coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen ion beam induced modifications on the properties of carbon quantum dots/TiO2 nanocomposite
coredata.dc:creator: Yashwanth, H.J.
Thorat, A.B.
Dahiwale, S.S.
Dhole, S.D.
Phase, D.M.
Bhoraskar, V.N.
Hareesh, K.
coredata.dc:description: The present study reports the modifying the properties of carbon quantum dots/titanium dioxide (CQDs/TiO2, termed as CT) nanocomposite by Nitrogen (N+) ion beam irradiation in the energy range of 10 keV–40 keV at a fluence of 3 × 1015 ions cm−2. Transmission electron microscopic results reveals the anchoring of (3–5) nm CQDs on TiO2 nanoparticles of size ∼25 nm. UV–Visible spectroscopic results show the decrease in the bandgap of CT nanocomposite after N+ ion irradiation. X-ray Diffractogram exhibits the shift in the planes of CT nanocomposite due to irradiation. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic results indicate the presence of nitrogen functional groups after irradiation. Raman spectroscopy results reveal the enhancement in disorder parameters due to irradiation-induced large number of small sp2 domains of carbon. Photoluminescence spectroscopy results show the decrease in emission intensity of CT nanocomposite after irradiation due to suppression in the recombination rate of photoexcited electron-hole pairs. The work function of CT nanocomposite decreases after ion irradiation indicating the shifting of Fermi energy level towards conduction band as studied by Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy. Photoelectrochemical measurement studies reveals that improvement in the photoelectrochemical performance of 40 keV N+ ion irradiated CT compared to TiO2.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen ion
Carbon quantum dots
TiO2
Electronic energy loss
Work function

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Irradiation and characterization of CT nanocomposite

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: KH acknowledges DST-SERB, Government of India (No. ECR/2017/002788 ) for financial assistance.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of synthesis of CT nanocomposite.
Schematic representation of low energy ion beam irradiation of CT nanocomposite.
(a,d) TEM, (b,c) HRTEM (e) SAED pattern (f) EDAX, (g–i) elemental mapping images of CT nanocomposite. The inset of Fig. 3(c) shows the IFFT of (101) and (002) planes of TiO2 and CQDs respectively.

(a) UV–Visible absorbance spectra, and (c) Tauc's plot of pristine and different energy nitrogen ion irradiated CT nanocomposite at 3 × 1015 ions/cm2; (b) UV–Visible absorption spectrum, and (d) Tauc's plot of pristine and 40 keV nitrogen ion beam irradiated CT nanocomposite at different fluences.
XRD pattern of pristine and irradiated CT nanocomposite.
(a) XPS full survey scan of 40 keV N+ CT nanocomposite; high resolution XPS of (b) C1s, (c) N1s (d) O1s and (e) Ti2p of TiO2 (f) Ti2p of 40 keV N+ CT nanocomposite.
Raman spectra of pristine TiO2, CT and 40 keV N+ irradiation CT.
Photoluminescence spectra at an excitation wavelength of 320 nm of CT nanocomposite at different energy from 10 keV to 40 keV.
Ultraviolet photoelectron spectra of TiO2, pristine and 40 keV nitrogen ion irradiated CT nanocomposites.

(a) Linear sweep voltammetry (b) Photocurrent response of TiO2, CQD/TiO2 and 40 keV N+ irradiated CQD/TiO2 electrode at a potential of 0.85 V (vs. Ag/AgCl). (c) Nyquist EIS plot of TiO2, CQD/TiO2 and 40 keV N+ irradiated CQD/TiO2 electrode under 150 W Xenon lamp (The supporting electrolyte was a 1 M aqueous solution of KOH).
The electronic energy loss (Se), nuclear energy loss (Sn) and Range (R) of nitrogen ions in CT nanocomposite calculated using SRIM-2008.
The chemical composition of Pristine CT and N+ irradiated CT nanocomposite.
EIS fitting parameters of as prepared TiO2, CT nanocomposite and 40 keV N+ ion irradiated CT nanocomposite.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen ion beam induced modifications on the properties of carbon quantum dots/TiO2 nanocomposite

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Manipal Institute of Technology, MAHE, Bengaluru, 560064, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Manipal Institute of Technology, MAHE, Bengaluru 560064, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present study reports the modifying the properties of carbon quantum dots/titanium dioxide (CQDs/TiO2, termed as CT) nanocomposite by Nitrogen (N+) ion beam irradiation in the energy range of 10 keV–40 keV at a fluence of 3 × 1015 ions cm−2. Transmission electron microscopic results reveals the anchoring of (3–5) nm CQDs on TiO2 nanoparticles of size ∼25 nm. UV–Visible spectroscopic results show the decrease in the bandgap of CT nanocomposite after N+ ion irradiation. X-ray Diffractogram exhibits the shift in the planes of CT nanocomposite due to irradiation. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic results indicate the presence of nitrogen functional groups after irradiation. Raman spectroscopy results reveal the enhancement in disorder parameters due to irradiation-induced large number of small sp2 domains of carbon. Photoluminescence spectroscopy results show the decrease in emission intensity of CT nanocomposite after irradiation due to suppression in the recombination rate of photoexcited electron-hole pairs. The work function of CT nanocomposite decreases after ion irradiation indicating the shifting of Fermi energy level towards conduction band as studied by Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy. Photoelectrochemical measurement studies reveals that improvement in the photoelectrochemical performance of 40 keV N+ ion irradiated CT compared to TiO2.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Titanium dioxide (TiO2) has attracted intense research interest in nanoscience and nanotechnology over the last few decades [1], due to its outstanding properties such as excellent optical transmittance, higher photoactivity, chemical stability [2–4], etc. Making it suitable for many photocatalysts applications like a solar cell, hydrogen production [5]. In spite of many studies on a various photocatalysts, TiO2 remains the most researched semiconductive material for photocatalytic and photoelectrochemical hydrogen production as its conduction band edge is compatible with redox potential of water [6]. Nevertheless, the quantum efficiencies of TiO2 in solar energy conversions are rather poor due to the fast recombination rate of photoinduced electron-hole pairs. Also, its wide bandgap of around 3.2 eV, limits its application in the visible region of the solar spectrum [6]. This drawback can be overcome by using dye sensitization, non-metal doping, noble metal loading, the addition of transition metals, scaffolding carbon quantum dots on TiO2 nanoparticles [7,8]. Since the discovery in 2004, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were progressed as an emergent class of nanomaterials [9], which have an intricate internal structure comprised of sp2 hybridized aromatic domain embedded in sp3 hybridized matrix. The coupling of TiO2 with CQDs has got attention because the valence band of the resulting composites will shift towards the Fermi energy level leading to a decrease in the band gap making it as an active material under visible light. Also, the created virtual energy levels above the valence band of TiO2 may suppress the recombination rate of photoinduced electron-hole pairs in TiO2 [7,10], which could enhance the properties of CT nanocomposite [11]. The properties of TiO2 based nanocomposites could be enhanced by the sonochemical method [12], Chemical vapor deposition [13], solvothermal method [14] and low energy ion irradiation method [15]. Among all, low energy ion irradiation method is regarded as a clean, efficient, and time-saving method [16], which could be used to dope [17] or modify the properties of nanocomposite to the requirement by creating defects [18]. It is also capable of control the ion concentration and distribution in the incident ion controlling ion fluence and energy in a nanocomposite [19]. In this context of ion beam irradiation of TiO2 nanostructures and its properties were studied by various research groups using different low energy ion beams. Sudhakar et al. [20], have reported Nitrogen ion implantation on TiO2 photoanode scaffolded quantum dots solar cells shows enhanced photovoltaic performance by 145% as compared bare TiO2. Verma et al. [21], have reported 500 keV Ar2+ ion irradiation on Au/TiO2 nanocomposites shows enhanced photocurrent density about 6 times higher than TiO2 due to the presence of plasmonic Au ions. The band gap of TiO2 nanoparticle was decreased from 3.2 eV to 2.9 eV using 8 MeV C+ ion implantation by Farooq et al. [22]. Mishra et al. [23] have irradiated graphene/TiO2 nanocomposite by Ag, Ni, Si ions demonstrated that electronic excitation causes modifications in the work function of the graphene/TiO2 nanocomposite. Kumar et al. [24] have reported the formation of intermediate states and free radicals between the valence band and conduction band of SnO2/TiO2 nanocomposites by Au ions irradiation. Siemek et al. [25] have observed the formation of ion track in the titanium after irradiation by 167 MeV Xe26+ ions. The 30 keV Ag + ion implantation on crystalline Ge substrate resulted in the formation Ge nanowire which was reported by Singh et al. [26]. Mohanty et al. [27] studied the photocatalytic methylene blue degradation of Ag/TiO2/PVA nanocomposite irradiated with 8 MeV Si ions and observed the enhanced photocatalytic activity by irradiated Ag/TiO2/PVA nanocomposite compared to pristine. Irfan et al. [28], have increased the efficiency of TiO2 based dye-sensitized solar cells by 3.26% using 500 keV Fe ions irradiation. Barman et al. [29] have observed the enhanced resistive switching behavior TiO2 thin films after 50 keV oxygen ion irradiation at fluence 5 × 1016 ions cm−2. The structural transformation of anatase TiO2 to brookite was observed by Bharathi et al. [30], during the irradiation of anatase TiO2 by 500 keV Ar2+ ions in the fluence ranging from 5 × 1014 ions cm−2 to 5 × 1016 ions cm−2. Zhan et al. [31] have observed the enhanced photoelectrochemical water splitting performance of TiO2 using Ar + ion irradiation and they argued that the creation of more oxygen vacancies in between the valence band and conduction band of TiO2 were responsible for the enhanced photoelectrochemical water splitting performance of TiO2 after irradiation. Nonetheless, the literature lacks the report on the modification of properties of CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites using low energy nitrogen ions.

Therefore, herein, the properties of CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites were modified using low energy N+ ion beam irradiation in the energy range from 10 keV to 40 keV at 3 × 1015 ions cm−2. The properties such as optical, structural, chemical, luminescence and photoelectrochemical properties of irradiated CT nanocomposite along with pristine sample were studied.

The schematic representation of synthesis of CT nanocomposite is as shown in Fig. 1 . Firstly, 1.1 g (0.4 mol) of glucose) was dissolved in 15 mL of double distilled (DI) water then 1 g of polyethylene glycol was added and stirred it for 30min until the clear transparent solution was obtained, Then, it was transferred into a 50 mL capacity of Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h in a Hot air oven and allowed to cool down to room temperature. Then, the obtained solution was centrifuged at 15,000 RPM for 10 min s to remove insoluble carbon precursors. Finally, supernant was dialyzed against DI water using 1 kDa dialysis membrane for 3 days by changing DI water every 24 h The dialyzed solution was further freeze-dried to get CQDs.

As prepared CQDs (5 wt%) were added to 10 mL of titanium tetra isopropoxide containing 5 mL of ethanol and 20 mL of double distilled water and stirred for 30 min. Then, the solution was placed in a Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 6 h. The attained solution was purified using 0.22 μm filter paper using a copious amount of double distilled water and ethanol. The so obtained precipitate was dried overnight at 60 °C in a hot air oven. The obtained CT nanocomposite was used for further irradiation purpose.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Irradiation and characterization of CT nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized CT nanocomposite was dissolved in the double-distilled water at a concentration of 0.5 mg mL−1 and sonicated for 2 h. Then, it was drop casted on to clean glass substrate and ITO substrate separately with an area of (1 × 1) cm2 and dried at room temperature. The nanocomposite was drop casted to get the optimum thickness of 12 nm, 20 nm, 29 nm, and 37 nm which was measured using an ellipsometer. These prepared samples were mounted in the ion beam irradiation chamber maintained at a vacuum of 4 × 10−6 torr. As mounted CT nanocomposites were irradiated by Nitrogen ion beam with different energies of 10 keV, 20 keV, 30 keV, and 40 keV at a fluence of 3 × 1015 ions cm−2. The electronic energy loss (Se), nuclear energy loss (Sn), and projected Range (R) of nitrogen ions in CT nanocomposite were calculated using SRIM-2008 software and are tabulated in Table 1 . The CT nanocomposite with thickness 12 nm, 20 nm, 29 nm, and 37 nm were respectively used to irradiate by 10 keV, 20 keV, 30 keV and 40 keV nitrogen ion beam at a fluence of 3 × 1015 ions cm−2. Also, CT nanocomposite was irradiated by a 40 keV nitrogen ion beam with different fluence of 3 × 1014 ions cm−2 to 3 × 1016 ions cm−2. The schematic representation of irradiation of CT nanocomposite using low energy ion beam is shown in Fig. 2 .

The particle size, energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy and elemental mapping of CT nanocomposite was studied using High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) of model JEOL/JEM 2100 at 200 kV. The magnification of the HR-TEM instrument is calibrated by recording the lattice images of the both known gold particles (standard) and specimen in the same field view. The UV–Visible absorption spectra of pristine and irradiated samples were characterized using JASCO, V-670 spectrophotometer. The structural properties of the irradiated samples was studied using Bruker AXSD8 X-ray diffractometer having CuKα radiation. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out to analyze the chemical bonding, and elemental composition present in the N+ irradiated CT nanocomposites using angle integrated photoelectron spectroscopy beamline integrated with Omicron EA-125 XPS analyser [32]. The base pressure during the spectra acquisition is 10−9 Torr. X-rays Mg Kα source was used operated at 1486 eV, toroidal grating monochromators (TGM) monochromator with total deflection 2θ = 162° was used during the experiment. The beam size was 1 mm (H) * 1 mm (V). The recorded spectra included core level spectra of nanocomposites, and the total acquisition time was 3 h. The spectra were acquired in sequential mode. The XPS instrument was calibrated using Ag, Au, and Cu foils as reference, which have had their genuine spectra determined with a metrology spectrometer. In order to avoid surface contamination, these foils were sputtered with Ag+ ions before measuring the spectra from 100 eV to 1600 eV. The ratio of recorded spectra of Ag, Au, and Cu, and standard reference spectra Ag, Au and Cu at 1 eV intervals provides the calibration. The complementary work function measurements were performed by Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) with an excitation energy as 23 eV, and toroidal grating monochromators (TGM) monochromator was used during the experiment. The binding energy scale was corrected by measuring all core-level spectra and work function (ΦSA) of the nanocomposites using XPS and UPS measurements respectively. Then, C1s peak position was corrected using the formula EB = 289.58–3.01 = 286.48, where 3.01 eV is the work function of the N+ irradiated CT nanocomposite calculated using ultraviolet photoemission spectroscopy. The calculated the required shift by taking the difference between measured C 1s binding energy and EB, and then applied the shift to all the core levels [33–35]. Raman spectrum was obtained using Renishaw Invia laser Raman microscope with a laser excitation wavelength of 532 nm. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra obtained using Perkin Elmer, LS 55 spectrophotometer at room temperature. AIPES beamline (BL-02) at Indus-1 synchrotron source was used to measure Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS). The photoelectrochemical (PEC) measurements were carried out using a three-electrode glass cell connected with an electrochemical workstation with a 150 W Xenon lamp. The N+ ion beam irradiated CT nanocomposite was used as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode and platinum wire as the counter electrode in 1 M KOH electrolyte. The photocurrents response was measured by illuminating the photoanodes (40 keV N+ irradiated CT nanocomposite, Pristine CT and TiO2) under 150 W Xenon lamp at the ambient condition in a dark chamber. Further, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out using the same electrochemical workstation in the frequency range from 1 MHz to 0.01 Hz.

Fig. 3 (a) and (d) showed the TEM images of CT nanocomposite revealing the scaffolding of CQDs of diameter (3–5) nm on TiO2 particles of size ∼25 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images CT nanocomposite (shown in Inset of Fig. 3(b–c)) depicted the d-spacing of 0.21 nm and 0.32 nm respectively corresponding to (002) crystal planes of attributed to carbon quantum dots and (101) crystal planes of TiO2 [36], which was further confirmed by Inverse Fast Fourier Transition (IFFT) profiles as shown in inset of Fig. 3(c). The SAED patterns of CT nanocomposites (Fig. 3(e)) confirms the presence of anatase phase of TiO2 and are in agreement with the XRD findings. The energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy analysis and mapping images of CT nanocomposites is as shown in Fig. 3(f) and (g–i) respectively. It can be observed from Fig. 3(f) that the peak observed for carbon, titanium and oxygen contents and elemental mapping images (Fig. 3(g–i)) revealed the presence and uniform distribution of carbon, oxygen and titanium in CT nanocomposite.

Fig. 4 (a) shows the UV–Visible absorption spectra of pristine and nitrogen ion beam irradiated CT nanocomposite at 3 × 1015 ions cm−2. It can be observed that the absorption edge (∼385 nm) of CT shifted towards a longer wavelength (i.e. redshift) with an increase in ion energy indicating the decrease in bandgap [37]. Fig. 4(b) displayed the Tauc's plot of pristine TiO2 and nitrogen ion implanted CT nanocomposite. It can be seen from the figure that the band gap of pristine CT nanocomposite was found to be 3.12 eV and it decreased to 2.17 eV for 40 keV nitrogen ion beam. Also, irradiated Nitrogen ions will provide a virtual energy level above the valence band of CT so that the bandgap of CT nanocomposites decreased [38]. The nitrogen ion beam loses its energy in CT nanocomposite by electronic energy loss as well as nuclear energy loss. Table 1 tabulates the electronic energy loss and nuclear energy loss of nitrogen ion beam in CT nanocomposite. It has been reported that electronic energy loss plays a major role in creating the substitutional energy levels/defect energy levels as it is due to the interaction of nitrogen ion beam with electrons of the nanocomposite. Therefore, the 10 keV N+ ion beam showed less decrease in the band gap. Nevertheless, the band gap decreased significantly for 20 keV, 30 keV, and 40 keV N+ ion beam irradiated CT nanocomposite. To study the effect of fluences at a particular energy, CT nanocomposite was irradiated by a 40 keV N+ ion beam at different fluences ranging from 3 × 1014 ions cm−2 to 3 × 1016 ions cm−2. The band gap of CT the nanocomposite decreased till 3 × 1015 ions cm−2 and then increased for 3 × 1016 ions cm−2 may be due to the degradation of the nanocomposite. It revealed that the tuning of the band gap of CT nanocomposite using low energy N+ ion beam requires a suitable fluence.

XRD patterns of TiO2 and CT nanocomposite before and after irradiation at different N+ ion energy at a fluence of 3 × 1015 ions cm−2 shown in Fig. 5 . The pristine TiO2 showed diffraction peaks around 25.6°, 38.1°, 48.7°, 54.3°, 55.5° and 63.2° corresponds to the lattice planes (101), (004), (200), (005), and (204) attributed to the anatase phase of TiO2 [39] which is in good agreement with JCPDS file No. 00-021-1272. The d-spacing of (101) plane of TiO2 was found to be 0.36 nm corroborating TEM results. The CQDs peak did not appeared in CT and N+ irradiated CT nanocomposite due to its less concentration and amorphous nature of carbon [40]. After irradiation, there is a decrease in the intensity of these peaks indicates that decrease in the crystallinity of the nanocomposite after N+ ions irradiation. Nevertheless, the peak of the lattice plane (101) of TiO2 shifted slightly towards a higher angle may be due to the doping of nitrogen in TiO2 resulting in a slight distortion of the plane.

The surface chemical properties of 40 keV N+ ion irradiated CT nanocomposite were studied by using XPS. The XPS survey scan of 40 keV N+ ion beam irradiated CT nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 6 (a) confirming the presence of C, N, Ti, and O respectively at 285 eV, 400.2 eV, 460 eV, and 532 eV. The chemical composition of pristine CT nanocomposite and N+ irradiated CT nanocomposite were calculated using XPS data by XPS CASA software and tabulate in Table 2 . It can be observed that the oxygen content reduced in CT nanocomposite after N+ irradiation indicating the creation of larger number of oxygen vacancies. It is known that the oxygen vacancies creates the energy levels of localized donor states below the conduction band of TiO2 and carbon quantum dots. Further, the irradiated CT nanocomposite showed the presence of nitrogen (3%) indicating the doping of nitrogen in TiO2 and carbon quantum dots. This doping of nitrogen provides an additional energy levels above the valence band of TiO2 and carbon quantum dots separately [38]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (shown in Fig. 6(b)) was deconvoluted to different peaks at 286.5 eV, 286.6 eV and 287.6eV which were attributed to C–C/CC, C–OH/C–O–C, CO, bonds respectively [41,42]. Similarly, the high-resolution XPS N1s (shown in Fig. 6(c)) spectra of the N+ ion irradiated CT nanocomposite were deconvoluted into three peaks centered at 398.3, 400.2, and 401.2 eV, corresponding to CN–C (pyridinic N), Ti-ON, and N–O groups respectively which confirms the doping of nitrogen in both carbon quantum dots as well as TiO2. Similarly, the high-resolution XPS of O1s of irradiated CT nanocomposite (displayed in Fig. 6 (d)) showed peaks at 531.41 eV and 532.54 eV attributed Ti–O and C–O–C bonds respectively [42]. The deconvoluted high-resolution XPS spectrum of Ti spectra of TiO2 is as displayed in Fig. 6(e) which exhibited peaks at 459.38 eV, 465.18 eV corresponding to Ti 2P3/2 and Ti 2P1/2 spin-split peaks respectively. Further, the deconvoluted high-resolution XPS spectrum of Ti spectra of 40 keV N+ irradiated CT nanocomposite displayed in Fig. 6(f), which showed peaks at 459.29 eV, 465.92 eV attributed to Ti 2P3/2 and Ti 2P1/2 spin-split peaks. Also, it can be observed that 40 keV N+ ion irradiated CT nanocomposites showed the slight shift in the Ti peak towards lower binding energy due to the lower electronegativity of nitrogen compared to that of oxygen. The introduction of nitrogen in titanium leads to increase in the electron density of the Ti atom owing to a partial electron transfer from nitrogen to titanium [20].

The Raman spectra of TiO2, CT, N+ ion irradiated CT nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 7 . The pristine TiO2 sample showed four distinct Raman peaks at 149 cm−1 (Eg), 395 cm−1 (B1g), 515 cm−1 (A1g) and 635 cm−1 (Eg) corresponding to vibrational modes of Anatase TiO2 phase [44]. Further, these peaks intensity improved for 40 keV N+ ion irradiated CT nanocomposite suggesting that redshift in the absorption edge of CT. Also, these peaks shift to a higher wavenumber for 40 keV N+ ion irradiated CT nanocomposites, this shift is due to non-stoichiometry in TiO2 crystal structure induced by the oxygen vacancies [45]. The small peak located at 350 cm−1 is assigned to O–O interactions consistent with TiO2 octahedral [46]. Also, Raman spectra of CT and 40 keV N+ ion irradiated CT nanocomposites shows additional peaks at 1380 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1 due to the D band and G band respectively attributed to the A1g k-point phonons breathing mode and sp2 atoms tangential stretching mode of carbon. Furthermore, the intensity ratio of D band to G band (ID/IG) of CT nanocomposite increased slightly after irradiation indicating the large number of small sp2 domains which were caused by the ion irradiation [47].

Fig. 8 depicted the PL emission spectra (at an excitation wavelength of 320 nm) of pristine and N+ ion irradiated CT nanocomposite with different. The pristine CT sample showed a PL peak at 382 nm which could be assigned to the fundamental band-to-band transition of TiO2. The intensity of this peak decreased with increased nitrogen ion energy indicating the lowering of recombination rate of photoinduced electron-hole pair [48]. The effective quenching of the photoluminescence can be occurred due to either electron being trapped by the oxygen vacancies created due to N+ ion irradiation or the hole being trapped by the irradiated nitrogen ions [43].

Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) was carried out to measure the work function of CT nanocomposites using the equation [49],
φ = h v −
|
E sec
−
E
F E
|
where hv = 23 eV, incident photon energy, E
sec is secondary emission onset and E
FE is Fermi edge. Fig. 9 displayed the E
sec and E
FE edge of pristine and 40 keV N+ ion beam irradiated CT nanocomposite along with TiO2. The work functions were found to be 4.3 eV, 3.43 eV and 3.01 eV respectively for TiO2, CT nanocomposite and 40 keV N+ ion beam irradiated CT nanocomposite. The decrease in the work function of N+ ion beam irradiated CT nanocomposite was due to the creation of irradiation-induced defect levels causing the upward shift of Fermi energy levels towards conduction band making the electron to get excited to conduction band easily. It is important to observe that pristine CT nanocomposite showed less work function compared to TiO2 due to energy levels created by CQDs.

The photoelectrochemical performances of TiO2, CT, 40 keV N+ ion irradiated CT nanocomposite are as shown in Fig. 9. The linear sweep voltammetry curves of nitrogen ion beam irradiated CT nanocomposite are displayed in Fig. 10 (a) and it can be found from the figure that the nitrogen ion beam irradiated CT nanocomposite showed a significant increase in current compared to CT and TiO2. It can be seen from the figure that the onset potentials of TiO2, CT, 40 keV N+ ion irradiated CT nanocomposite were found to be 0.59 V, 0.54 V, 0.51 V (v/s Ag/AgCl), i.e. the onset potential showed negative shift for nitrogen ion beam irradiated CT nanocomposite compared to CT nanocomposite and TiO2 implying that the more efficient charge separation of the photoanode [8,50]. The transient photocurrent responses for TiO2, CT, 40 keV N+ ion beam irradiated CT photoanode were investigated using the chronoamperometric measurement under chopped illumination conditions at a constant voltage of 0.8 V and is shown in Fig. 10(b). Upon illumination, N+ ion beam irradiated CT showed a significant increase in transient photocurrent (0.6 mA cm−2) compared to CT nanocomposite (0.4 mA cm−2) as well as pristine TiO2 (0.1 mA cm−2) due to the lowest bandgap of N+ ion irradiated CT. This indicates enhanced interfacial charge transfer kinetics between irradiated CT photoanode and the electrolyte, as well as efficient charge carrier separation which helps to boost photoelectrochemical water splitting performance [51].

The electrochemical impendence spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out to study interfacial charge transfer resistance (Rct) between the electrolyte and 40 keV N+ ion beam irradiated CT photoanode. The obtained Nyquist plots obtained for TiO2, CT nanocomposite and 40 keV N+ ion beam irradiated CT nanocomposite photoanodes under visible light illumination are displayed in Fig. 10(c). The diameter of the semicircle of EIS curve (corresponds to interfacial charge transfer resistance) for 40 keV N+ ion beam irradiated CT nanocomposite was much smaller than that of CT nanocomposite as well as pristine TiO2 which implies that efficient charge transfer with lower charge transfer resistance between 40 keV N+ ion beam irradiated CT photoanode and the electrolyte [52]. Further, impedance spectra were examined by the Randel circuit which exhibited in inset of Fig. 10(c). The obtained values of R1 (series resistance), RT (charge transport resistance) and RW (transfer resistance) between 40 keV N+ ion beam irradiated CT photoanode and the electrolyte are tabulated in Table 3 . It can be observed that the charge transport resistance and transfer resistance is less for irradiated CT. The lowest RT value observed for N+ irradiated CT photoanode confirms faster charge transfer and improved charge separation in N+ irradiated CT compared to CT and TiO2 photoanodes at the interface, which was due to decrease in surface recombination. The suppressed recombination rate of photogenerated charge carriers of N+ ion irradiated CT compared to CT and TiO2 is also corroborated by PL spectroscopic results discussed in the above section.

In Summary, the physico-chemical properties of CT nanocomposite were modified using nitrogen ion beams in the energy range from 10 keV to 40 keV. TEM results revealed the decoration of CQDs of size (3–5) nm on TiO2 nanoparticles of size ∼ 25 nm. The band gap of irradiated CT nanocomposite decreased due to the creation of energy levels above the valence band and oxygen vacancies below the conduction band, which further suppressed the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs as studied by PL spectroscopy. XPS results revealed the presence of nitrogen functional groups such as CN–C, Ti-ON, and N–O in irradiated CT nanocomposite indicating the doping of nitrogen in it. Raman spectroscopic results showed the formation of large number of small sp2 domains for irradiation. The work function of irradiated CT nanocomposite was found to be decreased suggesting the shifting of fermi energy level towards conduction band. The N+ ion irradiated CT nanocomposite showed enhanced photoelectrochemical performance due to the decrease in band gap, work function and recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. The results conclude that the low energy nitrogen ion beam could be utilized to make CT nanocomposite as a promising photoanode material for photoelectrochemical water splitting application.

H.J. Yashwanth: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. A.B. Thorat: Methodology, Investigation. S.S. Dahiwale: Resources, Methodology. S.D. Dhole: Resources, Methodology. D.M. Phase: Resources. V.N. Bhoraskar: Resources. K. Hareesh: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: KH acknowledges DST-SERB, Government of India (No. ECR/2017/002788) for financial assistance.
